name: Papyrus 45
significance: One of the Chester Beatty Papyri; contains portions of all four Gospels and Acts

name: Papyrus 52
significance: Earliest known New Testament manuscript fragment

name: Papyrus 66
significance: One of the two oldest substantial John manuscripts

name: Papyrus 75
significance: Co-oldest with P66 for John's opening chapters; closely related to Codex Vaticanus

name: Papyrus 90
significance: Among the oldest New Testament fragments

name: Codex Sinaiticus
significance: Most important complete New Testament manuscript

name: Codex Vaticanus
significance: Oldest nearly-complete New Testament codex

name: Codex Ephraemi Rescriptus
condition: Palimpsest — overwritten with Syriac text of Ephraem; partially recovered

name: Codex Bezae
significance: Bilingual Greek-Latin; Western text with many unique readings

name: Codex Regius
significance: Important witness for the ending of Mark

name: Codex Koridethi
repository: Tbilisi, Georgian National Centre of Manuscripts

name: Codex Purpureus Petropolitanus
repository: St. Petersburg (and scattered in other collections)
condition: Written on purple vellum in silver ink

name: Codex Petropolitanus
repository: St. Petersburg, National Library of Russia

name: Codex Vaticanus 354
significance: Precisely dated codex (949 AD)

name: Codex Rossanensis
condition: Illustrated purple vellum; does not contain John

name: Minuscule 1
significance: Key member of Family 1 (Lake Group)

name: Minuscule 13
significance: Key member of Family 13 (Ferrar Group)

name: Minuscule 33
significance: "Queen of the Minuscules" — closest to the Alexandrian text type

name: Minuscule 69
significance: Member of Family 13

name: Minuscule 118
significance: Member of Family 1

name: Minuscule 124
significance: Member of Family 13

name: Minuscule 131
significance: Member of Family 1

name: Minuscule 205
significance: Member of Family 1

name: Minuscule 209
significance: Member of Family 1

name: Minuscule 346
significance: Member of Family 13

name: Minuscule 543
significance: Member of Family 1

name: Minuscule 565
condition: Written on purple vellum in gold ink

name: Minuscule 788
significance: Member of Family 13

name: Minuscule 983
significance: Member of Family 13

name: Minuscule 1292
significance: Member of Family 13

name: Minuscule 1346
significance: Member of Family 13

name: Minuscule 1506
significance: Precisely dated codex (1320 AD)

name: Minuscule 1582
significance: Member of Family 1

name: Minuscule 2372
significance: Member of Family 13

name: Minuscule 2542
significance: Member of Family 1

name: Byzantine Minuscules (9th–12th century)
repository: Various repositories across Europe and the Middle East
significance: Approximately 600 manuscripts; the dominant manuscript tradition of the medieval Byzantine church

name: Byzantine Minuscules (13th–16th century)
repository: Various repositories across Europe and the Middle East
significance: 1,000+ manuscripts; late medieval copying tradition; basis of the Textus Receptus

name: Greek Lectionary Tradition
significance: ~2,280 manuscripts; liturgical books containing selected NT readings for church services; most contain John passages

name: Old Latin (Vetus Latina)
full_name: Vetus Latina — Multiple versions (a, b, c, d, e, ff2, q, r1)
significance: Earliest Latin translations, predating Jerome's Vulgate; witnesses to the 2nd–3rd century Greek text

name: Latin Vulgate (Jerome)
significance: Standard Bible of the Western church for over 1,000 years; 8,000+ manuscripts surviving

name: Syriac Sinaiticus
significance: Old Syriac version; one of two oldest Syriac Gospel manuscripts; discovered by Agnes Smith Lewis in 1892

name: Syriac Curetonian
significance: Old Syriac; discovered by William Cureton in 1842; contains partial Gospels

name: Syriac Peshitta
significance: Official Bible of Syriac-speaking Christianity; still in liturgical use today

name: Syriac Harklean
significance: Precise translation completed in 616 AD by Thomas of Harkel; noted for its literalism

name: Coptic Sahidic
significance: Earliest Coptic NT version; Upper Egyptian dialect; witnesses to early Alexandrian text

name: Coptic Bohairic
significance: Lower Egyptian dialect; still used in the Coptic Orthodox Church liturgy

name: Coptic Fayyumic
significance: Middle Egyptian Coptic dialect; partial NT

name: Armenian Version
significance: Translated by Mesrop Mashtots; called the "Queen of Versions" for its accuracy; 1,244+ manuscripts surviving

name: Georgian Version
significance: Translated from Armenian and Greek; key witness to early Caesarean text

name: Ethiopic (Ge'ez) Version
significance: Oldest Christian translation in sub-Saharan Africa; still in liturgical use in the Ethiopian Orthodox Church

name: Gothic Version (Wulfila)
significance: Translated by Bishop Wulfila, who created the Gothic alphabet specifically for this translation

name: Old Church Slavonic
significance: Translated by Cyril and Methodius for Slavic peoples; foundation of Slavic Christian literacy
